games.explanation: Baltimore is being treated like a coin-flip opponent here, and they simply are not that team anymore. Shane Baz has spent the year getting tattooed by right-handed bats, which is unfortunate timing given that Tampa's lineup happens to be stacked with righties who slug, and Aranda and Díaz against this exact profile is the kind of pairing that quietly decides games. Matz isn't a star, but he limits walks and induces enough soft contact to keep an Orioles lineup that's been merely fine from doing real damage. The Rays are the better team by a wide margin tonight, with a projected win rate closer to 65% than the 56% the number implies. At -126, you're paying fair-fight juice for a fight that isn't fair.

Seattle is being priced like the obvious side because of the logo on the jersey, but Emerson Hancock is the kind of starter who quietly hands games away to lineups that take pitches. He walks lefties at a double-digit clip and gives up a .391 xwOBA to that side of the plate, which is a problem when the White Sox can roll Vargas and Montgomery into the heart of the order. Burke isn't a star, but he misses enough bats to keep pace in a park that suppresses runs and under a closed roof that takes the crowd out of it. Getting plus money on the better-projected team in a pitcher-friendly environment is the entire angle.

Sale at this price assumes a clean, dominant night, and the Marlins lineup is actually built to be a nuisance to him. Miami runs out a stack of righties who don't chase or whiff much at the top of the order, exactly the profile that turns Sale's strikeout machine into a pitch-count grind. Junk isn't anyone's idea of a stopper, but the simulated game lands inside a run, and Atlanta's bats outside of Olson have been quietly mediocre all year. At +170, you're getting a coin-flip game priced like a mismatch, and a 44% shot at plus-170 is the entire argument.

A coin-flip price on the better team starting the better pitcher is the kind of thing the market is supposed to catch, and tonight it didn't. Connelly Early is still a small-sample arm, but the strikeout rate is genuinely loud and Wacha's been a hittable contact merchant against righty bats all season. Boston's right-handed core (Abreu, Contreras, Duran when the platoon tilts his way) is exactly the profile that's been punishing Wacha's stuff, and the Royals don't have the same edge in the other dugout. Getting even money on the side with the rotation advantage and the deeper lineup is the whole ticket.

The market keeps pricing Pittsburgh like the gap between these rosters doesn't exist, and that's the whole opening. Mlodzinski is a fine swing piece but he's also a righty who lets right-handed bats slug a .331 xwOBA against him, which is exactly the side of the plate Herrera and Burleson have been doing damage from all year. McGreevy isn't going to dominate anyone, but he doesn't have to when his offense is the better unit and Cruz, Lowe, and Reynolds all carry strikeout rates north of 26%. The sim sees a tight one and the Elo gap is enormous, yet you're getting a coin-flip price on the clearly better team. -116 on the side with the edge is the easiest part of the night.

Ohtani as a pitcher has been excellent, but the board is pricing him like he's untouchable against a Padres lineup that handles right-handed velocity as well as anyone in the league. The split that matters here is that his numbers against righties are good rather than otherworldly, and San Diego runs Tatis, Machado, and Sheets at him from that side. Randy Vasquez is not going to win a quality-of-arm contest, but he doesn't have to when the Dodgers offense gets four or five innings to do damage and the bullpen game is roughly a coin flip. At +150 on a matchup our numbers see closer to a pick'em, the discount on the underdog is doing the heavy lifting.

At +170, you're getting a coin-flip game priced like a mismatch, and a 44% shot at plus-170 is the entire argument.

Atlanta is the better team and Chris Sale is the better pitcher, and the market has priced this like that's the end of the conversation. It isn't, because run lines live and die on whether the favorite blows the game open, and Sale has been pitching to a sub-.270 xwOBA all year, which is exactly the profile that produces 3-1 ballgames, not 8-2 ones. Janson Junk is no one's idea of a stopper, but Miami's lineup is built around contact bats who don't strike out, and Sale's whiff rate against righties is his weakest split. The simulation has this as a coin flip on the actual scoreboard, which is absurd value when you're getting a run and a half. Pay -109 for the cushion and let the math do the rest.

A coin-flip price on the better team starting the better pitcher is the kind of thing the market is supposed to catch, and tonight it didn't.
